ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general sugarcane juice entry; the packaged/commercial variant is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers sugar cane juice generically; packaged commercial variant mapped to this code as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for packaged sugar cane drink; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packaged sugar cane drink in Singapore is sold in a 250 ml can or bottle, equivalent to approximately 250 g.

nutrient_source.title: Sugar Cane Drink (Packaged) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged sugar cane drink (cr_id D05010037). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; sugar cane juice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for packaged sugar cane drink found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 43 is derived from the closest analogous entry for fresh sugar cane juice reported in peer-reviewed literature (Foster-Powell et al. 2002, Am J Clin Nutr; Wolever et al. studies on sugarcane juice). Packaged product is assumed to have a similar or marginally higher GI due to processing; GI 43 used as a conservative estimate. GL per serving calculated as (43 × 8.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 250 g serving) / 100 = 8.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (8.3 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention, despite negligible sodium (2 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage; AHA 2018 guidelines identify SSBs as independently raising cardiovascular and blood pressure risk regardless of sodium content, and the 8.3 g/100 g sugar load exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 8.3 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre in liquid form causes rapid glucose absorption and significant postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely; liquid sugars are absorbed faster than solid food sugars, and even natural sugar cane juice causes clinically significant post-meal blood glucose spikes with no fibre to attenuate absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and elevated cardiovascular disease risk independent of total caloric intake
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this packaged sugar cane drink; the 8.3 g/100 g sugar content in liquid form contributes to dyslipidaemia and metabolic risk factors associated with CVD per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar content with no anti-inflammatory nutrients promotes systemic inflammation relevant to RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage; the 8.3 g/100 g sugar content promotes pro-inflammatory mediators and the drink provides no omega-3, antioxidants, polyphenols, or fibre to offset inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged sugar cane drink is a commercially processed, ready-to-drink beverage made from extracted sugar cane juice, sold in cans or bottles in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It is a popular convenience drink widely available in supermarkets, convenience stores, and vending machines. The drink has a light golden-yellow colour, a mildly sweet flavour characteristic of fresh sugar cane, and a thin, watery texture. Unlike freshly pressed sugar cane juice sold at hawker stalls, the packaged version undergoes heat treatment and may contain added preservatives or flavourings to extend shelf life. Nutritionally, it provides 34 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates comprising virtually all of its energy content at 8.3 g/100 g, entirely from sugars. Protein and fat are naturally zero as this is a sugar-based liquid beverage with no protein or fat-contributing ingredients. Dietary fibre is absent, and sodium is negligible at 2 mg/100 g. The absence of fibre means all sugars are rapidly absorbed, resulting in a fast glycaemic response. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Despite being perceived as a 'natural' drink, the sugar content and liquid delivery form make it metabolically equivalent to other SSBs. It is suitable for healthy individuals in moderation but should not be consumed regularly due to its high sugar load relative to its low nutritional density.

Can diabetics drink sugar cane juice?

Is sugar cane drink suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010037) with confidence 0.95. This is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from the HPB entry. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature as no direct packaged sugar cane drink GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero as this is a sugar-based liquid beverage containing no protein-contributing ingredients. fat_g is naturally zero as this product contains no fat-contributing ingredients; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. saturated_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and dietary_fibre_g are confirmed zero per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this packaged beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry. GI of 43 is an analogous estimate based on fresh sugar cane juice values from peer-reviewed literature (Foster-Powell et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2002); no direct GI for the packaged commercial variant was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving = (43 × 8.3 × 250) / 10000 = 8.9. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists specifically for packaged commercial sugar cane drink.
